大多数是法学教授 | 有时反主流 | 常常自由至上主义 | 始终独立
此刻你在想什么？
尤金·沃洛赫
2026年7月24日 凌晨3:00
下篇：特朗普实施大规模有害且非法的第301条款关税

尤金·沃洛赫是斯坦福大学胡佛研究所的托马斯·M·西贝尔高级研究员，同时也是加州大学洛杉矶分校法学院荣誉退休的加里·T·施瓦茨杰出法学教授和杰出研究教授。当然，他在这里的帖子（就像其他博主的观点一样）仅代表他自己的观点，不代表任何机构的立场。他也是"言论自由不沉默"播客的联合主持人。

articles.title: Trump Imposes Massively Harmful and Illegal Section 301 Tariffs
articles.content:
今天，特朗普政府宣布了大规模的新关税，表面上依据1974年《贸易法》第301条授权：

周四，特朗普政府最终确定了对数十个美国贸易伙伴征收的两位数关税，旨在重建今年2月被最高法院否决的全面关税措施。

这些新关税税率从10%到12.5%不等，此前进行了为期五个月的调查，审查贸易伙伴是否努力从供应链中清除强迫劳动产品。新关税将在临时性全球10%关税到期时生效。

从周五开始，17个贸易伙伴——包括加拿大、欧盟、印度尼西亚、英国和墨西哥——将面临10%的关税，另外还有10个国家同意通过与美国签署贸易协定来解决强迫劳动问题。

另有43个国家——包括日本、中国、韩国和澳大利亚——将面临12.5%的关税税率……

第301条关税可能旨在取代临时性的第122条关税，后者今天到期，并已于5月被美国国际贸易法院正确地裁定无效（尽管上诉诉讼仍在进行中，特朗普可能仍希望再次使用第122条）。新关税将不可避免地提高消费者价格、减少经济增长、对美国经济造成严重损害，并进一步毒害我们与盟友和贸易伙伴的关系。

正如我之前解释的，计划中的第301条关税之所以违法，部分原因与2月份最高法院在一起我参与诉讼的案件中否决的IEEPA关税相同。乔治城大学学者彼得·哈罗尔在此网站发表的一篇客座文章中概述了第301条关税违法的更多原因。与IEEPA不同，第301条确实授权某些关税。但它不允许这种大规模权力攫取。

我的卡托研究所同事斯科特·林西科姆——一位贸易政策专家——在今天发表的《Dispatch》文章中，详细解释了为什么所谓的支持第301条关税的强迫劳动“调查”是一场骗局，是大规模总统权力攫取的借口。正如他所指出的，“调查”的结论显然是预先设定的，所征收的关税与任何真正的强迫劳动问题没有真正联系，而且许多受到这些大规模新关税影响的国家实际上对强迫劳动产品的限制比美国本身更严格。他得出结论，如果法院维持这些关税，总统将拥有最高法院在IEEPA案件中拒绝赋予他的那种无限关税权力：

强迫劳动行动是对法律的明显滥用，是对过去美国政府做法——甚至在特朗普总统任下的严重偏离。无论从哪个合理角度看，这都不能被视为安装特朗普关税壁垒并保护其免受联邦法院另一次类似IEEPA式失败的笨拙方式。在后一种情况下，政府可能会成功：第301条比未经测试的IEEPA在法律上更持久，虽然强迫劳动行动显然站不住脚，但法院可能只是不愿意质疑总统的裁定和行动。我们拭目以待。

如果法院真的批准这些关税，其问题可能远远超出这个虚假案件本身。第301条可能成为美国贸易代表办公室可以对任何国家、以任何税率、出于任何原因和持续任何时间征收关税的方式，只要它满足了该法律的小型程序要求。案件的实质优点、机构的调查结果或其对公众意见的考虑实际上并不重要。只要说一个国家没有充分做你说有害的事情，然后在走过场的听证和评论后征收全面关税即可。就这样。

这正是法院通过IEEPA裁决所制约的开放式关税权力攫取，只是多了一些程序上的粉饰。在这种情况下，第301条将成为一个广泛的关税生成器，而不是国会认为设计的针对性工具，它肯定会被特朗普或任何想要因碳排放、劳工标准、人工智能监管或其他任何事情对贸易伙伴征收关税的未来总统所使用。今天为强迫劳动关税欢呼的共和党人应该考虑，当民主党人掌握关税大权时他们会作何感想。

正如我和彼得·哈罗尔在关于第301条关税的早期文章中所解释的，特朗普的政策违反了第301条本身的要求，也违反了重大问题原则，该原则要求国会在授权行政机构做出“具有重大经济和政治意义的决定”时必须“明确表态”。至少，第301条并没有明确授权总统基于虚假调查和可疑借口对几乎所有主要贸易伙伴发动大规模贸易战。

如果第301条确实授权了此行动，它将违反宪法禁止委托原则，该原则限制立法权向行政机构的委托。关税是国会的具体列举权力。虽然最高法院的禁止委托先例远非完全清晰，但去年FCC诉消费者研究公司的裁决认为，委托征收税款和其他财政罚款的权力必须有明确的“下限”和“上限”，并且“当机构行动将影响整个国民经济时，比处理狭窄的技术问题时需要更多的指导。”政府的第301条方法没有有意义的下限或上限。而所主张的权力显然是大幅影响“整个国民经济”的权力。

在IEEPA案件中，最高法院强调“总统没有权力对任何国家的任何产品、以任何税率、在任何时间内征收关税。”首席大法官罗伯茨进一步指出，虽然某些法规确实授予总统关税权力（他特别提到了第301条），“但当国会委托其关税权力时，它是在严格限制下这样做的。”

特朗普今天的权力攫取不尊重任何此类限制。我希望并期待它将在法庭上受到挑战。当这种情况发生时，法院应该像否决IEEPA和第122条关税一样将其驳回。

文章《特朗普实施大规模有害且违法的第301条关税》首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
Today, the Trump Administration announced massive new tariffs, supposedly authorized by Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974:

The Trump administration on Thursday finalized new double-digit tariffs on dozens of U.S. trading partners as it seeks to reconstitute sweeping duties struck down by the Supreme Court in February.

The new duties, which range from 10 to 12.5 percent, follow a five-month investigation into trading partners' efforts to root out products made with forced labor from their supply chains and are set to take effect just as a temporary global 10 percent tariff expires.

Starting Friday, 17 trading partners — including Canada, the European Union, Indonesia, the United Kingdom and Mexico — will face a 10 percent duty, along with another 10 countries that agreed to address forced labor through signed trade agreements with the U.S.

Another 43 other countries, including Japan, China, South Korea and Australia, will face a 12.5 percent tariff rate….

The Section 301 tariffs may be meant to replace temporary Section 122 tariffs, which expire today, and which were rightly invalidated by the US Court of International Trade in May (though appellate litigation continues, and Trump may yet hope to use Section 122 again). The new tariffs will predictably raise prices for consumers, reduce economic growth, inflict grave harm on the US economy, and further poison our relationships with allies and trading partners.

As I have previously explained, the planned Section 301 tariffs are illegal for some of the same reasons as the IEEPA tariffs invalidated by the Supreme Court in February, in a case I helped litigate. Georgetown University scholar Peter Harrell outlined some additional reasons why the Section 301 tariffs are illegal, in a guest post here at this site. Unlike IEEPA, Section 301 does authorize some tariffs. But it does not permit a massive power grab like this.

In a Dispatch article published today, my Cato Institute colleague Scott Lincicome - an expert on trade policy - explains in detail why the forced labor "investigations" supposedly justifying the Section 301 tariffs are a sham, and a pretext for a massive presidential power grab. As he notes, the conclusion of the "investigations" was clearly predetermined in advance, the tariffs imposed have no real connection to any genuine forced labor issues, and many of the countries subject to the massive new tariffs actually have tighter restrictions on goods produced by forced labor than the US itself does. He concludes that, if courts uphold these tariffs, the president would have the kind of unlimited tariff authority the Supreme Court denied him in the IEEPA case:

The forced labor action is a clear abuse of the law and a serious departure from past U.S. government practice—even under President Trump. By no reasonable measure can it be considered anything other than a ham-fisted way to reinstall Trump's tariff wall and protect it from another IEEPA-like defeat in federal court. In the latter case, the administration might be successful: Section 301 is more legally durable than the untested IEEPA and, while the forced labor action is obviously flimsy, a court might simply be unwilling to question the president's determinations and actions. We shall see.

If the courts do rubber-stamp these tariffs, their problems will likely extend well beyond just this sham case. Section 301 could become a way for USTR to tariff any country, at any rate, and for any reason and duration, as long as it checks the law's minor procedural boxes. The actual merits of the case, the quality of the agency's findings, or its efforts to consider public input won't really matter. Just say a country doesn't adequately do something you say is harmful and then apply blanket tariffs after meaningless hearings and comments. Voila.

This is precisely the open-ended tariff power grab the courts checked with their IEEPA rulings, just with a little more procedural window-dressing. In such a case, Section 301 would be a broad tariff generator instead of the targeted tool Congress thought it designed, and it'll surely be used by Trump or any future president who wants to tariff trading partners over carbon emissions, labor standards, AI regulation, or anything else. Republicans cheering the forced labor tariffs today should consider how they'll feel when a Democrat holds the tariff pen.

As explained in my and Peter Harrell's earlier posts on the Section 301 tariffs, Trump's policy violates the requirements of Section 301 itself, and also runs afoul of the major questions doctrine, which requires Congress to "speak clearly" when authorizing the executive to make "decisions of vast economic and political significance." At the very least, Section 301 does not clearly authorize the president to start a massive trade war against almost all our major trading partners on the basis of a sham investigation and dubious pretexts.

If, somehow, Section 301 does authorize this action, it would violate the constitutional nondelegation doctrine, which limits delegation of legislative power to the executive. Tariffs are a specifically enumerated congressional power. While the Supreme Court's nondelegation precedent is far from completely clear, last year's decision in FCC v. Consumers' Research held that delegations of the power to impose taxes and other financial levies must have a clear "floor" and "ceiling" and that "[t]he guidance needed is greater when an agency action will affect the entire national economy than when it addresses a narrow, technical issue" [quotation omitted]. There is no meaningful floor or ceiling under the administration's approach to Section 301. And the power claimed is clearly one that massively affects the "entire national economy."

In the IEEPA case, the Supreme Court emphasized that "the president does not have the power to "impose tariffs on imports from any country, of any product, at any rate, for any amount of time." Chief Justice Roberts went on to note that, while some statutes do grant the president tariff authority (among which he specifically cited Section 301), "[w]hen Congress has delegated its tariff powers, it has done so… subject to strict limits."

Trump's power grab today respects no such limits. I hope and expect it will be challenged in court. And when that happens, courts should strike it down, just like they did with the IEEPA  and Section 122 tariffs.

 

The post Trump Imposes Massively Harmful and Illegal Section 301 Tariffs appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Justice Kagan Rejects Charge that Supreme Court Is "Rubber Stamp" for Trump Administration
articles.content:
一些法律和政治评论员声称，最高法院一直在竭力讨好或支持特朗普政府。据Politico报道，大法官埃琳娜·卡根在第九巡回上诉法院司法会议上发表讲话时，驳斥了这种常见说法。

“我不认为最高法院是那种‘我们只是要盲目批准现任政府所做的一切’的法院。恰恰相反，”卡根周四在司法会议上表示，“我认为这种说法是对法院的误解。”

这位自由派大法官、奥巴马任命的卡根表示，以6比3的票数推翻特朗普关税政策的裁决是非同寻常的。“关税可能是这位总统最关键的政策议题。这是他竞选时的主张，是他关心的大事，”她说，“我不认为近期有太多这样的裁决——法院推翻对一任总统如此重要的政策。”

最高法院是保守派主导的，作出保守派裁决，但这并不能证明它受到特朗普总统的操控。“你必须面对这样一个事实：有一个保守派法院在做一些保守派法学家长期以来认为应该做的事情，我可以非常不喜欢这些做法。但我认为，这与成为现任政府的附庸完全是两回事，”她说。

卡根还表示，大法官们未受特朗普出席出生公民权案口头辩论的影响。该案对特朗普同样至关重要，但最高法院驳回了他的立场，甚至连持异议的大法官也对特朗普政府立场的影响范围提出了质疑。

“从法院的角度来看，这几乎是一个无关紧要的事件，”卡根在首次公开评论这一不寻常的场景时表示。“坦白说，法庭内的气氛与往常任何一天一样。”

卡根还指出，特朗普在两小时的庭审进行到一半时便离开了。“辩论时间很长。你真的会觉得他大概有其他事情要处理，”她说。

“所以，我不知道。我想他大概不会再来了，”她补充道，引发了观众的笑声。

这篇题为《大法官卡根驳斥最高法院是特朗普政府“橡皮图章”的指控》的文章首次发布于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
Some legal and political commentators claim that the Supreme Court has bent over backwards to appease or affirm the Trump Administration. In recent remarks before the Ninth Circuit Judicial Conference, as reported by Politico, Justice Elena Kagan rejected this common narrative.

From Politico:

"I don't see this as a court that is just like, 'We're just going to rubber stamp what the current administration does.' You know, quite the opposite," Kagan said to a judicial conference here Thursday. "I think that that is a bad rap." . . .

Kagan, a liberal justice and Obama appointee, said the 6-3 decision scuttling Trump's tariffs was extraordinary.

"Tariffs is probably the key policy issue for this president. I mean, something he campaigned on, he ran on, he cared an enormous amount about," she said. "I don't think that there are all that many decisions in the recent times, where a court strikes down such an important policy to a a sitting president."

That the Court is conservative--and reaches conservative decisions--is not evidence that it is under President Trump's sway.

"You just have to deal with the fact that there's a conservative court doing things that conservative jurists have long thought it appropriate to do, and I can really dislike that. But it has nothing to do, I think, with becoming the arm of this current administration," she said.

Kagan also said that the justices were unaffected by President Trump's decision to attend oral argument in the birthright citizenship case, another case of extreme importance to Trump in which the Court rejected his position, and even dissenting justices raised questions about the breadth of the Trump Administration's position.

"It was sort of like….from the court's point of view, a non-event," Kagan said in her first public remarks on the unusual spectacle. "Honestly, the vibes in the courtroom were the vibes in the courtroom on any other day." . . .

Kagan also noted that Trump left about halfway through the two-hour session. "It was a long argument. You really did kind of think he probably had other things to do," she said.

"So, I don't know. I think he probably won't come back," she added, prompting laughter from the audience.

The post Justice Kagan Rejects Charge that Supreme Court Is "Rubber Stamp" for Trump Administration appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: "There Is an Error in the Supreme Court's Analysis in Loper Bright."
articles.content:
在Loper Bright Enterprises诉Raimando案中，最高法院推翻了Chevron U.S.A.诉NRDC案的裁决。该裁决规定，联邦法院应当对行政机关关于模糊法规的合理解释给予尊重。但在Loper Bright案中，法院认定Chevron尊重原则与《行政程序法》不相容。正如首席大法官罗伯茨所解释的，《行政程序法》第706条规定，"审查法院"——而非其审查行为的机关——应当"决定所有相关法律问题"并"解释法定条款"。

Chevron案的意见书未尝试将其裁决与《行政程序法》的条款相协调。实际上，Chevron案的决定根本没有提及《行政程序法》。但在Hospital Menonita de Guayama诉NLRB案的异议脚注中，伦道夫法官讨论了这一Loper Bright案中的"错误"。该案涉及哥伦比亚特区巡回法院被最高法院指令重新考虑其先前依据Chevron案给予行政机关解释尊重的裁决。

Loper Bright案的分析存在错误。法院的大部分推理以及戈萨奇大法官的赞同意见都致力于批评Chevron案忽视《行政程序法》第706条。但这种批评缺乏依据，因为第706条不适用于Chevron案中EPA规则的司法审查。该条款不适用是因为Chevron案并非《行政程序法》审查案件。《清洁空气法》取代了《行政程序法》："第5篇第553至557条及第706条的规定，除本款明确规定外，不适用于本款适用的诉讼。"因此，Loper Bright案中发现的问题并非Chevron本身的问题，而是法院——包括最高法院——在后续案件中不加区分地采用Chevron公式的问题，在这些后续案件中第706条确实适用。

"There Is an Error in the Supreme Court's Analysis in Loper Bright."一文首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
In Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimando, the Supreme Court overturned Chevron U.S.A. v. NRDC's holding that federal courts should defer to reasonable agency interpretations of ambiguous statutory provisions. In Loper Bright, the Court concluded that Chevron deference was incompatible with the Administrative Procedure Act. As Chief Jsutice Roberts explained, Section 706 of the APA  provides "'the reviewing court'—not the agency whose action it reviews—is to 'decide all relevant questions of law' and 'interpret . . . statutory provisions.'"

The Chevron opinion made no effort to reconcile its holding with the language of the APA. Indeed, the Chevron decision did not mention the APA at all. But perhaps there was a good reason for that.

In a footnote to his dissent in Hospital Menonita de Guayama v. NLRB--a case in which the D.C. Circuit was instructed by the Supreme Court to reconsider its prior decision deferring to an agency interpretation under Chevron--Judge Ray Randolph addresses this "error" in Loper Bright.

There is an error in the Supreme Court's analysis in Loper Bright. Much of the Court's reasoning (and a part of Justice Gorsuch's concurrence) is devoted to criticizing the Chevron opinion for ignoring section 706 of the Administrative Procedure Act, 5 U.S.C. § 706. See, e.g., 603 U.S. at 397-400, 411-12; id. at 428 (Gorsuch, J., concurring). The Court's critique is unfounded. It is unfounded because section 706 of the APA did not apply to judicial review of the EPA rule at issue in Chevron. Section 706 did not apply because Chevron was not an APA review case. The Clean Air Act superseded the APA: "The provisions of section 553 through 557 and section 706 of Title 5 [the APA] shall not, except as expressly provided in this subsection, apply to actions to which this subsection applies." 42 U.S.C. § 7607(d)(1). The problem the Court identified in Loper Bright was not with Chevron itself, but with courts, including the Supreme Court, indiscriminately adopting the Chevron formula in later cases in which section 706 of the APA did apply.

The post "There Is an Error in the Supreme Court's Analysis in <i>Loper Bright</i>." appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: E.U. Fines Google $1 Billion and Orders It To Give Competitors Equal Treatment
articles.content:
欧盟委员会以违反数字反垄断法规为由，对谷歌处以两笔总额达10亿美元的罚款。这家科技巨头究竟犯了什么滔天大罪才招致如此巨额罚款？原因是谷歌设置了搜索引擎和谷歌应用商店，引导消费者购买谷歌自己的服务，欧盟委员会称这违反了《数字市场法》（DMA）。

委员会指控谷歌在搜索结果中不公平地推广自己的购物、酒店、交通和体育服务，同时限制其他应用开发者通过谷歌应用商店以外渠道做广告和完成交易。谷歌现在必须采取措施，“公平且非歧视地对待出现在谷歌搜索结果中的第三方服务”，并允许应用开发者“在技术层面和合同层面通过谷歌应用商店分发应用，自由地与用户沟通、推广优惠并签订合同，不仅限于谷歌应用商店内”。

欧盟委员会负责清洁、公正和公平转型的执行副主席特蕾莎·里贝拉在委员会新闻稿中表示：“谷歌未能有效遵守《数字市场法》，今天我们采取了决定性但平衡的执法行动来制裁这些违规行为。”

《数字市场法》于2023年5月生效，旨在“使数字领域的市场更加公平和更具竞争性”。它通过要求“提供所谓核心平台服务的大型数字平台（如在线搜索引擎、应用商店、通讯服务）”遵守一系列综合法规来实现这一目标。这些法规包括强制这些企业允许用户访问其生成的数据，并禁止它们偏袒自己的产品（谷歌正是因此被罚）。《数字市场法》针对19家公司制定，其中16家是美国企业。

里贝拉补充道：“最优秀的产品应该因为更好而成功，而不是因为归运营搜索引擎的公司所有。欧洲消费者有权从应用开发者那里了解在哪里可以获得最优惠的方案，即使应用商店所有者得不到分成。这是《数字市场法》的承诺——保护数字市场的公平、选择和创新，造福所有欧洲公民。”

这是欧盟数十年来对美国大型科技公司进行打击的最新罚款。去年，欧盟根据同一反垄断法对苹果处以5亿欧元（约合5.69亿美元）罚款，对Meta处以2亿欧元（约合2.27亿美元）罚款。谷歌一直是《数字市场法》执法的最大目标，该公司最近还在一起上诉中败诉，被处以创纪录的45亿美元反垄断罚款，原因是涉嫌通过安卓系统的主导地位阻碍竞争。据路透社报道，最新的处罚使近二十年来欧盟委员会对谷歌的罚款总额接近120亿美元。

虽然罚款对谷歌来说是坏消息，但对全球消费者来说也是坏消息，因为他们可能很快就会感受到美国关税措施的影响。

唐纳德·特朗普总统此前曾威胁要对这些欧洲法规和罚款，特别是那些他认为对美国企业有歧视性的法规和罚款，采取关税和其他报复措施。白宫2025年的一份备忘录指出，如果外国政府“实施歧视性”或“不成比例”的“罚款、处罚、税收或其他负担”，特朗普政府“将采取行动，实施关税并采取其他必要的报复措施，以减轻对美国的损害并修复由此产生的不平衡”。

实施报复性关税也会伤害美国消费者，而且几乎无法阻止布鲁塞尔的监管狂热者继续追查美国科技公司按照自己认为合适的方式运营。

欧盟委员会为何持续惩罚科技公司这个问题，涉及到欧洲对大型科技公司持敌对态度的本质。“无论线上还是线下，恐惧和贪婪都会导致大型强权企业想要更多权力，”时任欧盟委员会竞争事务专员的玛格丽特·韦斯塔格在2024年声明。她指控大型数字平台成为“既是运动员又是裁判”，将在线经济变成了“少数大企业的领域”。

这种对科技公司不友好的态度在最近一轮对谷歌的罚款中可见一斑。难怪成功创新的科技公司不选择在欧洲建立基地。

原文首发于Reason.com
articles.summary:
The European Commission has hit Google with two fines totaling $1 billion for breaking digital antitrust regulations. What egregious sin did the tech giant commit to warrant such hefty fees? Setting up the Google search engine and Google Play Store to encourage consumers to purchase Google's services, which the European Commission says violates the Digital Markets Act (DMA).

The Commission alleges that Google unfairly promoted its own shopping, hotel, transport, and sports services in search results while limiting other app developers' ability to advertise and complete purchases outside Google Play. Google now must take steps to "treat third-party services that feature on Google's search results in a fair and non-discriminatory manner" and allow app developers to distribute their apps via the Google Play Store "both technically and contractually, to freely communicate, promote offers and conclude contracts with users not only within but also outside the Google Play app store."

"Google has fallen short of effective compliance with the Digital Markets Act, and today we have taken decisive yet balanced enforcement action sanctioning these breaches," Teresa Ribera, executive vice president for Clean, Just and Competitive Transition, said in the European Commission's press release.

The DMA, which became applicable in May 2023, intends to "make the markets in the digital sector fairer and more contestable." It tries to do so by requiring "large digital platforms providing so called core platform services, such as online search engines, app stores, messenger services," to comply with a comprehensive list of regulations. These include forcing these firms to allow users to access the data they generate and prohibiting them from favoring their own product (which Google was fined for). The DMA was written with 19 companies in mind, of which 16 are American.

"The best products should succeed because they're better, not because they're owned by the company running the search engine," Ribera added. "And European consumers have a right to be told by app developers where to sign up to the best offers, even when the app store owner does not get a cut. This is the promise of the DMA, protecting fairness, choice and innovation in digital markets for the benefit of all European citizens."

This is the latest fine in the European Union's decades-long clampdown on American Big Tech. Last year, the E.U. fined Apple 500 million euros (about $569 million today) and Meta 200 million euros (about $227 million today) under the same antitrust law. Google has borne the brunt of DMA enforcement, having recently lost an appeal over a record $4.5 billion antitrust fine for allegedly thwarting competition through Android's dominance. The latest penalties bring the value of European Commission fines against Google to almost $12 billion over nearly two decades, according to Reuters.

While the fines are bad news for Google, they are also bad news for consumers worldwide, who may soon feel the effects of tariffs imposed by the United States.

President Donald Trump has previously threatened tariffs and other retaliatory measures in response to European regulations and fines, especially for those that his administration considers discriminatory toward American companies. A 2025 White House memorandum states that if a foreign government "imposes a fine, penalty, tax, or other burden" that is "discriminatory" or "disproportionate," the Trump administration "will act, imposing tariffs and taking such other responsive actions necessary to mitigate the harm to the United States and to repair any resulting imbalance."

The imposition of retaliatory tariffs would hurt American consumers too, and would do little to dissuade the regulatory zealots in Brussels from going after American tech companies for operating their companies as they see fit.

The question of why the European Commission consistently punishes tech companies speaks to the nature of Europe's adversarial attitude toward Big Tech. "Whether offline or online, fear, and greed, lead big and powerful companies to want more power," Margrethe Vestager, then the European Commission's competition chief, declared in 2024. She accused large digital platforms of becoming "the player and the referee" and turning the online economy into "the realm of the big few."

This tech-hostile attitude was seen in the most recent round of fines against Google. It is no wonder that successful and innovative tech companies choose not to base themselves in Europe.

The post E.U. Fines Google $1 Billion and Orders It To Give Competitors Equal Treatment appeared first on Reason.com.
